moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the queen's pawn, which is one of the most flexible and solid first moves in chess. This central advance immediately stakes a claim to the e5 and c5 squares, giving us excellent control over the center. The Torre Attack that we're heading into is a system opening that's particularly appealing because it leads to similar pawn structures and piece placements regardless of how Black responds. This makes it an excellent choice for players who want to focus on understanding typical middlegame plans rather than memorizing countless variations.

Black develops the knight to its most natural square, attacking our d4 pawn and preparing to contest the center. This is one of the most common responses to d4, as it develops a piece while immediately putting pressure on our central pawn. Our opponent is keeping their options open - they could transpose into various defenses like the King's Indian, Queen's Indian, or Nimzo-Indian depending on how we continue. The knight on f6 also supports a potential ...e5 pawn break in the future, which would give Black excellent central counterplay.

We develop our knight to f3, defending the d4 pawn while developing toward the center. This knight placement is incredibly flexible - it controls the important e5 and h4 squares, supports a potential e4 pawn advance, and keeps our options open for various systems. By playing Nf3, we're not committing to any particular pawn structure yet, which is one of the key ideas behind the Torre Attack. The knight also prepares us to castle kingside quickly, ensuring our king's safety before launching any aggressive operations in the center or on the kingside.

Black plays the solid ...e6, supporting the center and preparing to develop the light-squared bishop. This move creates a small pawn chain with the potential ...d5 advance, and it's a hallmark of many classical defensive setups. Our opponent is opting for a solid, positional approach rather than sharp tactical complications - they're preparing to develop their pieces harmoniously while maintaining a solid pawn structure. The e6 pawn also opens up the diagonal for the dark-squared bishop, though it does somewhat limit the scope of the light-squared bishop on c8.

Here we play the signature move of the Torre Attack, developing our dark-squared bishop to g5 and pinning the knight on f6 to the queen. This is the key idea of our system - we're putting immediate pressure on Black's kingside and forcing them to make a decision about how to deal with the pin. The bishop on g5 eyes the important e7 and h6 squares, and it works beautifully with a potential Nbd2-e4 maneuver to create kingside pressure. This pin is more than just annoying - it can become tactically significant if Black castles kingside, as the knight becomes genuinely pinned to the king rather than just the queen.

Black immediately challenges our bishop with ...h6, forcing us to make a decision about where to retreat. This is the classical and most straightforward way to deal with the pin - our opponent doesn't want to allow us to maintain the pressure on the f6 knight. While ...h6 does weaken Black's kingside slightly by creating holes on g6 and creating potential tactical motifs later, it's still considered the most principled response. We now have several options: we can retreat to h4 (maintaining the pin), exchange on f6 (damaging Black's pawn structure), or go to f4 (preparing central play with e3 and c3). Each choice leads to different types of middlegame positions with distinct strategic themes.
